title: Cat Constant Sneezing Fits: Causes, Urgency, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Frequent sneezing in cats can indicate infections or irritants. Learn when sneezing fits are a concern and how to triage your pet's symptoms.
content_html:
TL;DR: Constant sneezing in cats usually signals respiratory irritation or infection and typically requires a veterinary visit within 24 to 48 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is struggling to breathe or has stopped eating.

What causes persistent sneezing in my cat?

It can be very distressing to see your feline friend experiencing constant sneezing fits. In cats, this is typically a sign of upper respiratory irritation. Common causes include viral infections such as Feline Herpesvirus or Calicivirus, bacterial infections, inhaled irritants like strong perfumes or dust, or even a foreign object like a blade of grass stuck in the nasal passage. While an occasional sneeze is normal, frequent fits suggest an underlying issue that needs professional attention.

Is constant sneezing in cats a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for constant sneezing is generally considered medium.
  • While rarely life-threatening, sneezing fits require a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent worsening.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is struggling to breathe or breathing with an open mouth.
  • Immediate attention is necessary if your cat has completely stopped eating.

How can photos and videos of my cat's sneezing help a veterinarian?

  • Record a video of the sneezing fit so the vet can determine if it is a true sneeze, a cough, or a "reverse sneeze."
  • Take clear photos of nasal discharge to show the color and consistency, such as whether it is clear, yellow, or bloody.
  • Visual evidence helps differentiate between simple allergies and serious infections, leading to a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Frequent sneezing in cats, particularly in fits, can be a sign of acute rhinitis or feline viral rhinotracheitis (FVR). Acute rhinitis is characterized by nasal discharge, sneezing, pawing at the face, respiratory stertor, open-mouth breathing, inspiratory dyspnea, lacrimation, and conjunctivitis. Sneezing is an attempt to clear the upper airways of discharge or exudate. In FVR, onset is marked by fever, frequent sneezing, conjunctivitis, rhinitis, and salivation, with excitement or movement inducing sneezing. Nasal and ocular discharge starts serous but becomes mucopurulent, potentially leading to depression and anorexia. Severely debilitated cats can develop ulcerative stomatitis and keratitis. Given that sneezing fits can indicate potentially serious underlying conditions like FVR or complicated bacterial infections, veterinary consultation is warranted.

Chapter: Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1481)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human allergy medicine for sneezing?
faqs.answer: No, never give your cat human medication without consulting a vet. Many over-the-counter human drugs contain ingredients that are highly toxic to cats.

faqs.question: What does yellow or green nasal discharge mean?
faqs.answer: Thick, colored discharge often indicates a secondary bacterial infection that likely requires prescription antibiotics from your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Why is my cat sneezing blood?
faqs.answer: Bloody discharge, or epistaxis, can be caused by severe inflammation, a foreign body, or more serious conditions. This should be evaluated by a vet as soon as possible.
